programs.name: Medicaid for Seniors/Disabled
programs.tagline: Comprehensive health coverage including nursing home care and home-based services for Alaskans 65+ or disabled with limited income.

localEntities.name: Local Division of Public Assistance (DPA) offices statewide

programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ or disabled and lives on a fixed income, Alaska Medicaid can cover their complete healthcare costs — including nursing home care, home health services, prescription drugs, and Medicare premiums. The program has different pathways depending on whether your parent needs long-term care: income limits range from $1,845/month for basic coverage to $2,982/month for nursing home or home-based waiver services.

Because Alaska Medicaid coordinates with Medicare for dual-eligible seniors, your parent won't pay Medicare premiums, deductibles, or copays. The program also covers services Medicare doesn't — like personal care, home modifications, and adult day programs through Home and Community Based Services (HCBS) waivers. Asset limits are strict: $2,000 for single applicants, $3,000 for couples, though the family home is exempt (subject to estate recovery after death).

The application process is lengthy and complex, with multiple program pathways and functional assessments required for long-term care services. HCBS waivers may have waitlists due to limited slots, while nursing home placement typically has no waitlist but depends on provider availability.

summary: Income under $1,845-$2,982/month depending on care level

exemptAssets: Burial plots and pre-need burial contracts

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: For long-term care services, must meet Nursing Facility Level of Care (a clinical assessment showing you need daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or other activities of daily living)
otherRequirements: Must be U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen
Must be Alaska resident
Cannot be eligible for Medicare in some pathways
Must apply for SSI if income is below SSI standards

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at my.alaska.gov or call your local Division of Public Assistance office — expect a lengthy process that can take weeks to months.

steps.title: Use the pre-screening tool
steps.description: Visit health.alaska.gov to determine which Medicaid pathway fits your parent's situation and get the correct phone number for your area.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect income proof, asset statements, medical records, and identification before starting your application.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Apply online at my.alaska.gov, call your local DPA office for an appointment, or mail/fax completed forms to your regional office.

steps.title: Complete level of care assessment
steps.description: If applying for long-term care services, a clinical assessment will determine if your parent meets Nursing Facility Level of Care requirements.

steps.title: Follow up on processing
steps.description: Stay in contact with your assigned caseworker and respond quickly to any requests for additional documentation.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Lengthy and complicated process that can take weeks to months
applicationGuide.waitlist: HCBS Waivers may have waitlists due to limited slots; Nursing Home placement typically has no waitlist but depends on provider availability
applicationGuide.tip: Use the pre-screening tool at health.alaska.gov first to identify which program pathway applies to your parent's situation — this can save significant time.

documentsNeeded: Social Security cards for applicant and spouse
Medicare card (both Part A and Part B)
Birth certificate or passport for proof of age
Proof of Alaska residency (utility bill, lease, or state-issued document)
Most recent Social Security award letter or disability determination
Pension or retirement income statements
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Investment documents (stocks, bonds, annuities, trust agreements)
Property documents (deeds, tax statements, mineral rights)
Vehicle titles and registration for all vehicles
Life insurance policies with face values and cash surrender values
Pre-need burial contracts or irrevocable burial trusts
Medical records documenting disability or need for long-term care
Documentation of medical expenses paid out-of-pocket (last 3 months)
Legal documents if someone else will represent the applicant

contacts.label: Alaska DPA Medicaid Information
contacts.phone: Call number provided through pre-screening tool
contacts.description: Program-specific number based on your location and situation

contacts.label: Alaska 2-1-1
contacts.description: Free helpline for all social services and benefit programs

applicationNotes: Multiple Medicaid pathways exist with different income, asset, and functional requirements — use the pre-screening tool to identify the right program before applying
Asset limits are very low ($2,000 individual) — consider Medicaid planning strategies if your parent has excess resources
The family home is exempt from asset limits but subject to estate recovery after death — consult an elder law attorney about protections
Working disabled individuals have a separate Buy-In program with higher income allowances but may require premium payments

contentSections.title: Alaska Medicaid Pathways
contentSections.description: Different programs serve different needs and have different requirements:

tiers.name: Regular Medicaid (ABD via APA)
tiers.benefits: Doctor visits, hospital care, prescriptions, basic medical services

tiers.name: Long-Term Care Medicaid
tiers.benefits: All regular Medicaid plus nursing home care and HCBS waiver services

tiers.name: Working Disabled Buy-In
tiers.benefits: Full Medicaid coverage with possible premium payments

contentSections.title: Estate Recovery
contentSections.content: Alaska Medicaid may recover costs from your parent's estate after death, including placing a lien on their home. Consult an elder law attorney about protections for the family home and other assets.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if it's worth more than the asset limit?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary home is exempt from Medicaid's asset limits regardless of value. However, Alaska may recover Medicaid costs from the home's sale after your parent's death through estate recovery.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is slightly over the limit?
faqs.answer: Your parent may still qualify through a spend-down process, paying medical expenses until their income falls below the limit. Long-term care Medicaid also has higher income limits ($2,982/month) than regular Medicaid ($1,845/month).

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist for home-based services really?
faqs.answer: HCBS waiver waitlists vary by program type and region, potentially lasting several months to years. Nursing home placement typically has no waitlist, but finding an available bed in a preferred facility may take time.

faqs.question: Can I apply for Medicaid on behalf of my elderly parent?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can apply on behalf of elderly or disabled relatives. You may need to provide legal documentation showing you have authority to act on their behalf, such as power of attorney.

faqs.question: Can my parent have both Medicare and Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Yes, many Alaska seniors are 'dual eligible' for both programs. Medicaid pays Medicare premiums, deductibles, and copays, plus covers services Medicare doesn't, like long-term care and personal care services.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent gets better and no longer needs nursing home level care?
faqs.answer: Medicaid eligibility is reviewed annually, and more frequently if circumstances change. If your parent's functional status improves, they may transition from long-term care Medicaid to regular Medicaid, which has lower income limits.

faqs.question: Can my parent apply for Medicaid and Adult Public Assistance at the same time?
faqs.answer: Yes, and in many cases getting approved for APA automatically qualifies your parent for Medicaid. The programs are closely linked and can be applied for together through the same process.

faqs.question: What if my parent owns a second car or vacation property?
faqs.answer: Medicaid counts second vehicles and vacation homes as countable assets toward the $2,000 limit. Your parent would need to sell these assets or convert them to exempt resources before qualifying.

programs.name: Home and Community-Based Services Waiver
programs.tagline: Comprehensive home care services to help your parent avoid nursing home placement while staying Medicaid-eligible.

localEntities.name: Division of Senior and Disabilities Services (SDS), Division of Public Assistance (DPA)

programs.intro:
If your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or has dementia but wants to stay home, Alaska's Home and Community-Based Services Waiver can provide comprehensive care coordination, adult day services, respite care, home modifications, specialized medical equipment, and even residential supported living — all while keeping them Medicaid-eligible.

Because the waiver covers coordinated services under one program, your parent won't need to piece together care from multiple providers — one care coordination team manages everything from nursing visits to meal delivery to transportation. The state operates five different waivers: ALI serves adults 65+ or those 21-64 with physical disabilities, while other waivers focus on developmental disabilities or children with complex medical needs.

Your parent must qualify for both Medicaid income limits and meet Nursing Facility Level of Care (a clinical assessment confirming they need substantial daily assistance). Some waivers have waitlists, with IDD selecting only 50 people annually from their waitlist.

summary: Needs daily help with 3+ activities like bathing, dressing, or medication
Must qualify for nursing facility level of care
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must meet Nursing Facility Level of Care through functional assessment (Consumer Assessment Tool) — requires substantial medical care or extensive help with 3+ daily activities like eating, mobility, toileting, or combinations with behavior/cognition issues
otherRequirements: Alaska resident
Medicaid eligible through Division of Public Assistance
Cannot have transferred assets under fair market value within past 60 months (Look-Back Rule applies)

applicationGuide.summary: Contact Division of Public Assistance for Medicaid eligibility first, then a care coordinator assists with the nursing facility assessment — state schedules assessment within 30 days.

steps.title: Apply for Medicaid eligibility
steps.description: Contact Division of Public Assistance (DPA) through health.alaska.gov to establish Medicaid eligibility. You must meet both income and asset limits determined through DPA interview and application.

steps.title: Request nursing facility level assessment
steps.description: Once Medicaid-eligible, contact Division of Senior and Disabilities Services (SDS) Intake/Assessment Unit. A care coordinator will be assigned to help with your application.

steps.title: Complete functional assessment
steps.description: State schedules Consumer Assessment Tool evaluation within 30 days of care coordinator submitting completed application. Assessment can be conducted in-person or by video.

steps.title: Develop support plan
steps.description: If approved for Nursing Facility Level of Care, your care coordination team creates individualized support plan specifying services like adult day care, respite, home modifications, or specialized equipment.

applicationGuide.processingTime: State schedules NFLOC assessment within 30 days of care coordinator submitting completed application
applicationGuide.waitlist: IDD waiver selects 50 people annually from waitlist; ISW waiver has waitlist when participant limit reached; ALI and APDD typically no waitlist
applicationGuide.tip: Apply for Medicaid first through DPA — you cannot get waiver services without Medicaid eligibility established.

documentsNeeded: Valid government-issued photo ID or driver's license
Proof of Alaska residency (utility bill, lease agreement, or state-issued document)
Social Security cards for all household members
Social Security award letters or disability determination letters
Medicare card (both parts A and B)
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Investment documents (stocks, bonds, annuities, trust agreements)
Property documents (deeds, property tax statements)
Vehicle titles and registration
Life insurance policies with cash values
Pension or retirement income statements
Medical records documenting need for daily assistance
Current medication list and prescribing physician information
Documentation of current care arrangements or services
Pre-need burial contracts or irrevocable burial trusts

contacts.label: Alaska 2-1-1
contacts.description: Free helpline for all social services and program information

contacts.label: Division of Public Assistance
contacts.description: Medicaid eligibility determination and application

contacts.label: Division of Senior and Disabilities Services
contacts.description: HCBS waiver applications and care coordination

applicationNotes: Multiple waivers available — ALI serves adults 65+ or those 21-64 with physical disabilities, while other waivers focus on developmental disabilities or complex medical needs
Must meet both Medicaid financial eligibility AND nursing facility level of care — failing either requirement results in denial
Services depend on local provider availability and may be limited in rural areas
Look-Back Rule applies — assets transferred for less than fair market value within 60 months create penalty periods

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house and still qualify for the waiver?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary residence is exempt from asset limits if your parent intends to return home. However, if they transferred the house for less than fair market value within the past 60 months, this triggers the Look-Back Rule and creates a penalty period that delays eligibility.

faqs.question: What's the difference between the ALI waiver and other waivers?
faqs.answer: ALI serves adults 65+ or those 21-64 with physical disabilities and offers services like adult day care, respite, home modifications, and residential supported living. APDD serves adults 21+ with developmental disabilities, while IDD, ISW, and CCMC focus on developmental disabilities or children with complex medical needs.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist really?
faqs.answer: ALI and APDD waivers typically have no waitlist. IDD waiver selects only 50 people annually from their waitlist, creating potentially years-long waits. ISW waiver creates a waitlist when participant limits are reached. Waitlist length varies significantly by waiver type.

faqs.question: Can I apply for the waiver while my parent is in the hospital?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can start the Medicaid application process while your parent is hospitalized. Hospital discharge planners often help initiate waiver applications. Contact Division of Public Assistance immediately to begin Medicaid eligibility determination.

faqs.question: What if we live in a rural area with limited providers?
faqs.answer: Services vary by community based on provider availability, and rural areas may have fewer options. Your care coordinator will work within available local providers to develop your support plan, but some services may not be accessible in remote locations.

faqs.question: Can family members provide some of the care services?
faqs.answer: The waiver covers formal services through approved providers, but family caregivers can continue providing informal care alongside waiver services. Respite services specifically give family caregivers breaks from caregiving responsibilities.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's care needs change after enrollment?
faqs.answer: Your care coordination team regularly reviews and updates the support plan based on changing needs. Services can be added, modified, or reduced as your parent's functional abilities or medical conditions change over time.

faqs.question: Can my parent have both Medicare and waiver services?
faqs.answer: Yes, the waiver works alongside Medicare and Medicaid. Medicare covers medical services while the waiver covers long-term care services like personal care, respite, and home modifications that Medicare doesn't provide.

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
programs.tagline: All-in-one medical care and support services to keep your parent out of a nursing home — if a provider operated in Alaska.
programs.intro:
If your parent is 55+ and needs extensive daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management, PACE would provide all their medical care, prescription drugs, adult day programs, home care, and social services under one coordinated team — with no copays or deductibles. Because PACE covers everything from primary care to hospitalization to personal care aides, your parent wouldn't need to coordinate between separate doctors, insurance plans, or service providers.

The major barrier: No PACE programs currently operate in Alaska. While PACE serves over 50,000 seniors nationally and prevents nursing home placement for 90% of participants, Alaska families cannot access this program. You'll need to explore Alaska's Medicaid waiver programs or private care coordination instead.
programs.savingsSource: Comprehensive care service with no copays for enrollees

summary: Need nursing home level of care
Able to live safely in community
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be certified as needing nursing home level of care (extensive help with 2-3 activities of daily living like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, or walking)
otherRequirements: Live in a PACE service area (none exist in Alaska)
Able to live safely in the community with services
Cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage or hospice
US citizen or 5-year legal resident for Medicare

applicationGuide.summary: No PACE programs operate in Alaska — contact Alaska Medicaid to ask about updates or alternative long-term care programs.

steps.title: Contact Alaska Medicaid
steps.description: Call (907) 465-3300 to ask about PACE program development or alternative long-term care services in Alaska

steps.title: Explore waiver programs
steps.description: Ask about Alaska's Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services waivers as alternatives to PACE

steps.title: Get nursing home assessment
steps.description: If your parent needs extensive care, request a Nursing Facility Level of Care assessment through Alaska Medicaid

applicationGuide.tip: While waiting for PACE availability, consider private geriatric care managers who can coordinate multiple services similarly to PACE teams.

contacts.label: Alaska Medicaid Services
contacts.description: State Medicaid office for long-term care programs and PACE updates

contacts.label: Alaska 2-1-1
contacts.description: Free helpline for all social services and care coordination resources

applicationNotes: PACE programs require state approval and significant local investment — Alaska may develop programs in the future
Private pay PACE enrollment (around $4,000-$6,000/month nationally) would be available if a provider existed
Alaska's geographic challenges and small population make PACE financially difficult compared to urban areas
relatedPrograms: Alaska Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services

faqs.question: Why doesn't Alaska have any PACE programs?
faqs.answer: PACE requires significant upfront investment and a large enough population to be financially viable. Alaska's geographic spread and smaller senior population make it challenging for organizations to establish sustainable PACE centers. Contact Alaska Medicaid at (907) 465-3300 to ask about future development plans.

faqs.question: Can my parent get similar services through other Alaska programs?
faqs.answer: Yes, Alaska's Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services waivers provide some similar supports like personal care, adult day services, and care coordination. However, these don't include the comprehensive medical integration that makes PACE unique. Call (907) 465-3300 to explore waiver options.

faqs.question: What if we moved to another state with PACE?
faqs.answer: Your parent would need to establish residency in a PACE service area and go through the enrollment process in that state. Each PACE organization serves a specific geographic area, so you'd need to research which programs serve your target location and have availability.

faqs.question: How much would PACE cost if my parent paid privately?
faqs.answer: Nationally, private pay PACE enrollment averages $4,000-$6,000 per month, which covers all medical care, prescriptions, adult day programs, and home services. This is often less expensive than coordinating separate private services, but no Alaska providers exist to offer this option.

faqs.question: Can I apply now and wait for a program to start?
faqs.answer: No, there's no waiting list for non-existent programs. Alaska would need to approve and license a PACE organization first. Stay informed by checking with Alaska Medicaid annually about program development, or sign up for updates on their website.

faqs.question: What's the nursing home level of care assessment like?
faqs.answer: A state assessor evaluates whether your parent needs extensive help with activities of daily living like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, or walking. This same assessment qualifies for both PACE (if available) and Medicaid long-term care waivers. Request one through Alaska Medicaid at (907) 465-3300.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
programs.tagline: Help paying Medicare premiums, deductibles, and copays if your parent's income is below $2,245/month

localEntities.name: Local medical assistance offices throughout Alaska

programs.intro:
If your parent struggles to pay Medicare premiums, deductibles, or copays, three Medicare Savings Programs can help. QMB (Qualified Medicare Beneficiary) covers all Medicare out-of-pocket costs for individuals earning up to $1,663/month. SLMB and QI programs cover Medicare Part B premiums ($202.90/month in 2026) for those with slightly higher incomes up to $2,245/month.

Alaska residents get higher income limits than the lower 48 states because of the higher cost of living. All three programs automatically qualify your parent for Extra Help with prescription drug costs — paying no more than $12.65 per medication in 2026. Because these programs coordinate all Medicare payments, your parent won't receive separate bills for covered services.

QI has limited annual funding and fills up on a first-come, first-served basis. Your parent must already be enrolled in Medicare Part A to apply.
programs.savingsSource: Part B premiums plus potential deductibles and copays coverage

exemptAssets: Life insurance with cash value below $1,500
Some Native corporation stocks held by Alaska Native people

otherRequirements: Must be enrolled in Medicare Part A
Must be Alaska resident
SLMB and QI require both Medicare Part A and Part B
QI: Cannot receive QI benefits if you qualify for Medicaid

applicationGuide.summary: Apply through your local medical assistance office by phone at 1-877-486-2048, with processing time not specified.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect Medicare cards, income documentation, bank statements, and proof of Alaska residency before starting your application.

steps.title: Contact your local medical assistance office
steps.description: Call 1-877-486-2048 or visit your local office in person. Phone applications are accepted.

steps.title: Choose your program tier
steps.description: QMB covers all Medicare costs but has the lowest income limit ($1,663/month). SLMB and QI cover Part B premiums only but allow higher incomes up to $2,245/month.

steps.title: Submit complete application
steps.description: Provide all documentation at once to avoid delays. For QI program, apply early in the year due to limited funding.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Not specified — contact local office for current timeframes
applicationGuide.waitlist: QI program only: Limited funding, first-come first-served until annual appropriation runs out. Priority given to previous year recipients.
applicationGuide.tip: Apply for QI early in the calendar year since funding runs out annually on a first-come, first-served basis.

documentsNeeded: Medicare Part A enrollment card
Medicare Part B card (for SLMB and QI applications)
Proof of Alaska residency (utility bill, lease, or state-issued document)
Social Security award letter or benefit statement
Recent pay stubs or employment income documentation
Pension or retirement income statements
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Investment statements (stocks, bonds, annuities)
Life insurance policies with cash surrender values
Vehicle titles and registration documents
Property deeds or tax statements
Burial fund documentation or pre-need contracts
Documentation of Native corporation stocks (if applicable)
Tax returns from previous year
Any other asset or income documentation

contacts.label: CMS Medicare Savings Programs
contacts.description: National line for QMB program enrollment verification

contacts.label: Alaska Medical Assistance
contacts.description: Apply for all three Medicare Savings Programs

applicationNotes: QI program funding runs out annually — apply in January or February for best chance of acceptance
Alaska income limits are significantly higher than the lower 48 states, so don't use national eligibility calculators
All three programs automatically include Extra Help for prescription drug costs — this benefit is often overlooked
Income limits include a built-in $20/month disregard, so the stated limits already account for this exclusion

content.covers: Medicare Part A and B premiums, deductibles, coinsurance, and copayments
content.additional: Automatically qualifies for Extra Help with prescription drugs

content.covers: Medicare Part B premiums only ($202.90/month in 2026)
content.additional: Automatically qualifies for Extra Help — pay no more than $12.65 per drug

content.covers: Medicare Part B premiums only ($202.90/month in 2026)

faqs.question: Can my parent apply for QMB and SLMB at the same time?
faqs.answer: No, these are three separate programs with different income tiers. Your parent will be evaluated for the highest benefit level they qualify for. If their income is below $1,663/month, they'll get QMB which covers the most costs.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is just over the limit one month?
faqs.answer: Alaska evaluates income monthly and includes a $20 general income disregard. The income limits shown already include this disregard. Temporary income spikes may not disqualify your parent permanently.

faqs.question: Does my parent's house count toward the $9,950 asset limit?
faqs.answer: No, the primary home or mobile home is completely exempt from asset limits. One vehicle, household items, wedding rings, and burial plots up to $1,500 also don't count.

faqs.question: How early should we apply for the QI program?
faqs.answer: Apply in January or February. QI has limited annual funding and operates first-come, first-served. Once the money runs out, even eligible people are denied until the next year.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly parent?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can apply on behalf of Medicare beneficiaries. Contact the local medical assistance office at 1-877-486-2048 to discuss the process and any additional authorization needed.

faqs.question: What happens to Extra Help if my parent loses Medicare Savings Program eligibility?
faqs.answer: Extra Help with prescription drug costs is tied to Medicare Savings Program enrollment. If your parent loses eligibility, they'll need to reapply for Extra Help separately or face higher prescription costs.

faqs.question: Are Alaska Native corporation dividends counted as income?
faqs.answer: Some Native corporation stocks held by Alaska Native people are exempt from asset limits, but dividend payments may count as income. Contact your local medical assistance office for specific guidance on Alaska Native benefits.

faqs.question: What if my parent qualifies for Medicaid — can they still get QI?
faqs.answer: No, QI program specifically excludes people who qualify for Medicaid. However, QMB and SLMB may still be available. Medicaid may provide more comprehensive coverage than QI anyway.

programs.name: Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP)
programs.tagline: Monthly grocery assistance for seniors on fixed incomes — up to hundreds per month on an EBT card.

localEntities.name: Local Division of Public Assistance offices statewide

programs.intro:
If your parent is on a fixed income, SNAP can provide monthly grocery money loaded onto an EBT card that works like a debit card at most stores and farmers markets. Because Alaska has expanded beyond federal rules, seniors and disabled adults can qualify with higher incomes than other states — up to $3,258/month for a single person.

The benefit amount depends on your parent's net income after deductions for medical expenses over $35/month and housing costs. Households with all adults 60+ or disabled get simplified rules: higher asset limits ($4,500 instead of $3,000), longer certification periods (36 months), and no recertification interviews unless there's a problem.

Because this is a federal program, your parent keeps SNAP benefits if they move between states. Alaska administers the program through local Division of Public Assistance offices statewide.
programs.savingsSource: Monthly EBT benefits vary by household size and net income

countedAssets: Cash and bank accounts over limits
exemptAssets: Burial plots and pre-need burial contracts
One vehicle per household or vehicles used for work

otherRequirements: Must live in Alaska
Adults 18-54 without dependents must work, train, or volunteer 20+ hours/week (exemptions for 60+ and disabled)
Cannot have quit a job without good cause

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through MyAlaska portal, by phone at 800-478-7778, or at local DPA offices — processing time varies by office.

steps.title: Choose your application method
steps.description: Apply online at MyAlaska (health.alaska.gov/dpa), call the Virtual Contact Center at 800-478-7778, or visit your local Division of Public Assistance office in person.

steps.title: Complete the application
steps.description: Provide information about everyone in your household, income, expenses, and assets. For households where all adults are 60+ or disabled, you'll be enrolled in ESAP (Elderly Simplified Application Project) for streamlined processing.

steps.title: Submit required documents
steps.description: Upload documents through MyAlaska's secure portal, mail them to your local DPA office, or bring them in person. Include proof of income, expenses, residency, and identity.

steps.title: Complete eligibility interview
steps.description: DPA will schedule a phone or in-person interview to review your application. ESAP households may skip this step unless there are questions about eligibility.

steps.title: Receive your EBT card
steps.description: If approved, your EBT card will be mailed within 7-10 days. Benefits are loaded monthly and can be used at most grocery stores, farmers markets, and authorized retailers.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Varies by office and application completeness
applicationGuide.tip: If all adults in the household are 60+ or disabled, mention ESAP when applying for simplified processing with 36-month certification periods.

documentsNeeded: Valid government-issued photo ID for applicant
Social Security cards for all household members
Proof of Alaska residency (utility bill, lease, or state-issued document)
Most recent Social Security award letter or pension statements
Pay stubs from last 30 days (if working)
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Rent receipt or mortgage statement
Recent utility bills (electric, gas, water, phone)
Medical bills and receipts over $35/month (for 60+ or disabled)
Proof of medical insurance premiums paid
Child support payments made or received
Vehicle registration and titles
Life insurance policies with cash value information
Documentation of any other income sources

contacts.label: Alaska Virtual Contact Center
contacts.description: SNAP applications and general questions

contacts.label: Food Bank of Alaska
contacts.description: Application assistance and outreach support

applicationNotes: ESAP (Elderly Simplified Application Project) applies automatically if all adults in your household are 60+ or disabled — this means 36-month certification periods and no recertification interviews unless issues arise
Alaska's income limits are higher than federal minimums due to state expansion — don't assume you're ineligible based on other states' rules
Medical expense deductions over $35/month can significantly increase benefit amounts for households with members 60+ or disabled
If your household includes someone under 60 who isn't disabled, work requirements may apply to that person even if others are exempt
relatedPrograms: Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if it's worth more than the asset limit?
faqs.answer: Yes. The primary home and lot are completely exempt from SNAP asset limits, regardless of value. Your parent can own a million-dollar home and still qualify if their income and other assets meet the limits.

faqs.question: What if my parent is over the income limit but has high medical expenses?
faqs.answer: They may still qualify. Alaska allows households with members 60+ or disabled to deduct medical expenses over $35/month from their income. High prescription costs, insurance premiums, or medical bills can bring them under the income threshold.

faqs.question: Can I apply for SNAP on behalf of my elderly parent?
faqs.answer: Yes, if your parent authorizes you as their representative. You'll need to complete an authorized representative form during the application process. DPA can conduct the eligibility interview with you instead of your parent.

faqs.question: How much will my parent actually receive each month?
faqs.answer: It depends on their net income after deductions. Generally, every $100 increase in net income reduces benefits by about $30. Minimum benefit amounts vary by household size, and seniors often receive more due to medical and shelter deductions.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent moves to assisted living?
faqs.answer: SNAP eligibility depends on how meals are provided. If the facility provides all meals as part of the fee, your parent likely won't qualify. If they prepare their own meals or buy their own food, they can usually keep SNAP benefits.

faqs.question: Can my parent use SNAP for grocery delivery or meal kit services?
faqs.answer: Yes, many grocery stores that accept SNAP now offer delivery services that work with EBT cards. Some meal kit services also accept SNAP. However, prepared hot foods and restaurant meals generally aren't covered.

faqs.question: How long does SNAP certification last for seniors?
faqs.answer: If all adults in the household are 60+ or disabled, certification lasts 36 months under Alaska's ESAP program (through November 2029). Regular households typically recertify every 12 months.

faqs.question: What changes must we report to keep benefits?
faqs.answer: For ESAP households (all adults 60+ or disabled), you only need to report: if household composition changes so not all adults are 60+ or disabled, if an adult starts working, or lottery/gambling winnings of $4,500 or more. Other changes can wait until recertification.

programs.name: Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)
programs.tagline: Get help paying heating bills and preventing utility shut-offs if your parent is on a fixed income.

localEntities.name: State of Alaska Division of Public Assistance (statewide)
Aleutian Pribilof Islands Association (serves 10 specific communities)
Local WIC offices and senior centers

programs.intro:
If your parent struggles to pay heating bills or electric bills in Alaska, LIHEAP provides direct payment to energy vendors and crisis assistance when utilities are shut off or fuel runs low. The program helps with heating fuel, firewood, electric bills, and emergency situations when households have less than 5 gallons of heating fuel or face shut-off notices.

To qualify, your parent's household income must be at or below $2,443/month for one person or $3,303/month for two people (150% of Federal Poverty Level in 2026). They must also have at least $200 in out-of-pocket heating costs per year. Because Alaska has multiple program providers including state offices and tribal organizations, eligibility rules and benefit amounts can vary significantly by location and provider — some tribal programs exclude Social Security income from calculations, potentially increasing benefits for seniors.
programs.savingsSource: Financial assistance varies by household income, size, housing type, and geographic location

summary: Income at or below 150% Federal Poverty Level
May need Alaska Native/American Indian status for tribal programs
otherRequirements: Must have at least $200 in out-of-pocket heating costs per year
Cannot live in certain subsidized housing (varies by provider)
Some tribal programs require Alaska Native or American Indian documentation
Must live in provider's service area

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through the state website or contact your local provider — applications are processed within 45 days.

steps.title: Determine your provider
steps.description: Contact the State of Alaska Division of Public Assistance at liheap@alaska.gov or check if your community has a tribal provider like Sitka Tribe of Alaska or Native Council of Bethel.

steps.title: Check application window
steps.description: Application periods vary by provider. The state program accepts applications through August 2026. Some providers like THRHA accept applications November 1st for seniors 60+ and disabled clients, December 1st for general public.

steps.title: Submit application and documents
steps.description: Apply online at the Alaska Division of Public Assistance website, visit a local Public Assistance office, WIC office, or senior center, or contact your tribal provider directly.

steps.title: Wait for processing
steps.description: Complete applications are processed within 45 days for most providers, or within 30 days for Sitka Tribe of Alaska.

applicationGuide.processingTime: 45 days for complete applications (30 days for Sitka Tribe of Alaska)
applicationGuide.waitlist: Application windows are time-limited and vary by provider — seniors 60+ and disabled individuals may have priority application periods starting November 1st
applicationGuide.tip: If your parent receives SNAP benefits, bring the award letter showing countable income — this can speed up income verification.

documentsNeeded: Social Security cards for all household members listed on application
Head of household's government-issued photo ID
Last 30 days of income verification (pay stubs if employed)
Most recent Social Security, SSI, APA, TANF, or general assistance award letter
Recent SNAP (food stamps) award letter showing countable income
Complete current income tax return (all pages) if self-employed
Zero income statement for any household member 18+ claiming zero income
Recent heating bill (fuel, electric, or other heating costs)
Recent electric bill
Tribal card, tribal enrollment letter, or Certificate of Indian Blood (CIB) if applying through tribal provider
Proof of fishing income (settlement printouts covering last 12 months) if applicable

contacts.label: Alaska LIHEAP Email
contacts.description: Email liheap@alaska.gov for general program questions and provider information

contacts.label: Sitka Tribe of Alaska
contacts.description: Tribal LIHEAP provider serving Sitka area

contacts.label: Tanana Housing and Referral Agency
contacts.description: LIHEAP provider with priority windows for seniors 60+ and disabled clients

applicationNotes: Seniors 60+ and disabled individuals often get priority application windows starting November 1st, while general public applications may not open until December

Some tribal providers exclude Social Security income from calculations, which can significantly increase benefit eligibility for seniors — ask about this when choosing between state and tribal applications
If your parent lives in subsidized housing through ASHA, AVCP, or BNC, they may be ineligible for some provider programs
Crisis assistance for shut-off notices or households with less than 5 gallons of heating fuel may be available outside regular application windows

faqs.question: Can my parent apply through both the state and tribal providers to get more assistance?
faqs.answer: No, households can only receive LIHEAP assistance from one provider per program year. However, you should compare options because tribal providers may have different income calculation methods that could result in higher benefits.

faqs.question: What if my parent's Social Security income puts them over the income limit?
faqs.answer: Some tribal providers like the Aleutian Pribilof Islands Association exclude Social Security income from their calculations, while the state program counts it. If your community has both options, check with the tribal provider first.

faqs.question: My parent lives in subsidized housing — are they automatically ineligible?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily, but it depends on the provider and housing type. Some programs exclude residents in ASHA, AVCP, or BNC housing, while others may still provide assistance. Contact your local provider to verify eligibility.

faqs.question: What counts as the required $200 in annual heating costs if my parent lives in an apartment?
faqs.answer: This includes electric bills used for heating, payments for heating fuel, firewood purchases, or any out-of-pocket costs for keeping the home warm. Even apartment dwellers usually meet this requirement through electric heating or space heaters.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly parent who has dementia?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can apply on behalf of elderly parents who cannot manage their own affairs. You'll need to provide your parent's documents and may need to show legal authority to act on their behalf.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent gets a utility shut-off notice after the application deadline?
faqs.answer: Contact your provider immediately about crisis assistance, which may be available outside regular application windows for emergencies like shut-off notices, medical needs, or households with less than 5 gallons of heating fuel.

faqs.question: How much assistance will my parent actually receive?
faqs.answer: Benefit amounts vary based on household income, size, housing type, heating vendor, and geographic location. The program doesn't publish specific dollar amounts because they're calculated individually for each household's situation.

faqs.question: My parent is Alaska Native but not enrolled in a tribe — which provider should we use?
faqs.answer: Non-enrolled tribal citizens in some communities must apply through the State of Alaska rather than tribal providers. Contact both to determine which option is available and offers better benefits for your parent's situation.

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program
programs.tagline: Get up to $4,000 in free home weatherization services to reduce your parent's energy bills

localEntities.name: Association of Alaska Housing Authorities providers

programs.intro:
If your parent's household income is below $39,100 (single person) or $52,860 (couple), they may qualify for up to $4,000 in free weatherization services to reduce energy costs. The program provides furnace repairs or replacement, insulation, new windows and doors, programmable thermostats, and other energy-saving improvements at no cost.

Because the program gives priority to households with someone 60+ or disabled, your parent may move ahead of other applicants. However, homes that received weatherization services in the last 15 years are not eligible. You must apply through your regional provider — RurAL CAP in Anchorage, Tlingit-Haida Regional Housing Authority in Juneau, or other local agencies — not directly through the state.

The work can include everything from basic caulking and weather stripping to complete furnace replacement, depending on what your parent's home needs most. Both homeowners and renters qualify, and there are no asset limits.

summary: Income at or below area median income limits
Home not weatherized in last 15 years
Priority for age 60+, disabled, or households with children under 6
otherRequirements: Home cannot have received weatherization services from any program in the last 15 years
Cannot have received AHFC Home Energy Rebate after May 1, 2008
Must apply through regional provider for your area

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your regional weatherization provider directly — processing times vary by provider and priority level.

steps.title: Find your regional provider
steps.description: Visit ahfc.us/efficiency/weatherization to find the provider for your area. In Anchorage, contact RurAL CAP. In Juneau, contact Tlingit-Haida Regional Housing Authority at 907-780-6868.

steps.title: Request application
steps.description: Call or visit your regional provider to get the weatherization application form. Each provider has their own form (WX-ANC Application for RurAL CAP, Weatherization Assistance Application for others).

steps.title: Submit completed application
steps.description: Return the completed application with all required income documentation and proof of homeownership or lease agreement. All adults 18+ must sign with Social Security numbers.

steps.title: Wait for priority-based selection
steps.description: Your application will be reviewed based on priority (elderly, disabled, families with young children get preference). If selected, a contractor will assess your home's weatherization needs.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Not specified - varies by provider and priority level
applicationGuide.waitlist: Priority-based selection may result in wait times for non-priority households
applicationGuide.tip: Emphasize if your parent is 60+, disabled, or has high energy costs relative to income — these factors increase priority for selection.

documentsNeeded: Photo ID for all adults 18 and older
Proof of income for all household members from last 30 days (pay stubs, tax returns, W-2s)
Social Security award letters (SSI, SSDI, or SSA statements)
Bank statements for all accounts
Native dividend documentation if applicable
Disability income letters
Proof of homeownership (deed, mortgage statement, or property tax bill)
Lease agreement if renting
Recent utility bills showing energy costs

contacts.label: AHFC Weatherization Info
contacts.description: Visit website to find regional providers

contacts.label: Tlingit-Haida Regional Housing Authority (Juneau)
contacts.description: Weatherization services for Juneau area

applicationNotes: Each regional provider has slightly different application forms and processing procedures — contact the provider in your area
Priority goes to elderly (60+), disabled individuals, and families with children under 6 — mention these factors when applying
If your parent received any weatherization services since 2009, they may be ineligible — check with the provider about previous work
All adults in the household must sign the application and provide Social Security numbers — missing signatures delay processing
relatedPrograms: Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)

faqs.question: Can my parent apply if they had some weatherization work done years ago but aren't sure when?
faqs.answer: Contact your regional provider with your parent's address and they can check their records. If any weatherization work was done through a government program in the last 15 years, your parent won't qualify. However, work done by private contractors or utility companies doesn't count against this rule.

faqs.question: What if my parent rents and the landlord won't give permission?
faqs.answer: Renters need landlord permission for weatherization work, but landlords often agree because the improvements increase property value at no cost to them. Your regional provider can help explain the benefits to reluctant landlords. If the landlord still refuses, your parent unfortunately cannot participate.

faqs.question: How do I know if my parent will get selected since it's priority-based?
faqs.answer: Households with members 60+, disabled individuals, or children under 6 get priority, followed by those with the highest energy burden relative to income. Your regional provider can give you a better sense of wait times and your parent's likelihood of selection based on their specific situation.

faqs.question: Can my parent get both weatherization and LIHEAP energy assistance?
faqs.answer: Yes, these are separate programs and your parent can receive both. However, they cannot get weatherization if they received an AHFC Home Energy Rebate after May 1, 2008. Check with your provider about any other program conflicts.

faqs.question: What exactly will they do to my parent's home?
faqs.answer: The contractor will first do an energy assessment to identify the most cost-effective improvements. This could include furnace repairs or replacement, insulation, caulking, weather stripping, programmable thermostats, door and window repairs, or ventilation improvements. The work is limited to $4,000 per unit for buildings with 1-4 units.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly parent?
faqs.answer: Your parent must be involved in the application process and sign the forms, but you can help them complete the paperwork and communicate with the provider. If your parent has cognitive issues, contact the provider about accommodations or whether a power of attorney allows you to sign on their behalf.

programs.name: State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP)
programs.tagline: Free one-on-one help navigating your parent's Medicare options, appeals, and cost-saving programs.

localEntities.name: Alaska Medicare Information Office (Anchorage-based, statewide service)
Local counseling sites via state partnerships

programs.intro:
If your parent is approaching Medicare eligibility or struggling to understand their current coverage, Alaska's SHIP program provides free, personalized counseling to help them make informed decisions. Trained counselors help with Medicare Parts A, B, C, and D, Medigap policies, Medicare Savings Programs, Extra Help for prescription costs, and Medicaid applications.

Because SHIP counselors are not affiliated with insurance companies, they provide unbiased guidance tailored to your parent's specific health and financial situation. The service is available to anyone eligible for Medicare (typically age 65+ or under 65 with certain disabilities), plus family members and caregivers who need help understanding options on their loved one's behalf.

summary: Family members and caregivers also qualify
otherRequirements: Medicare eligibility (current or soon-to-be)
Alaska residents have priority but no strict residency requirement

applicationGuide.summary: Call the Alaska Medicare Information Office at 1-800-478-6065 for immediate counseling — no application needed.

steps.title: Call the helpline
steps.description: Contact 1-800-478-6065 (toll-free in Alaska) or 907-269-3680 (Anchorage direct). TTY users call 1-800-770-8973. Counseling is available immediately.

steps.title: Schedule your session
steps.description: Choose in-person, phone, or virtual counseling. Sessions are one-on-one and tailored to your parent's specific Medicare questions.

steps.title: Gather Medicare documents
steps.description: Have your parent's Medicare card, current plan details, prescription list, and income information ready if discussing cost-saving programs.

steps.title: Get ongoing support
steps.description: Return for additional counseling as needed — there's no limit on sessions and no enrollment required.

applicationGuide.tip: Contact SHIP counselors 3 months before your parent becomes Medicare eligible to explore all options before enrollment deadlines.

contacts.label: Alaska Medicare Information Office
contacts.description: Primary SHIP counseling line (toll-free in Alaska)

contacts.label: Anchorage Direct Line
contacts.description: Direct number for Anchorage-based counseling

contacts.label: TTY Service
contacts.description: TTY line for hearing-impaired callers

faqs.question: Can I get SHIP counseling for my parent even if I'm not on Medicare myself?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members and caregivers can access SHIP counseling to get help understanding Medicare options for their loved ones. Counselors will explain coverage options, costs, and enrollment procedures so you can help your parent make informed decisions.

faqs.question: Will SHIP counselors help if my parent already has Medicare and wants to switch plans?
faqs.answer: Absolutely. SHIP counselors help with plan comparisons during Open Enrollment (October 15 - December 7) and can explain Special Enrollment Periods if your parent qualifies due to life changes. They'll review your parent's current coverage against available options based on their specific medications and doctors.

faqs.question: How is SHIP different from talking to insurance company representatives?
faqs.answer: SHIP counselors are independent and not affiliated with any insurance company, so they provide unbiased information about all available options. Insurance company representatives can only discuss their own plans, while SHIP counselors compare all Medicare options available in your area.

programs.name: Meals on Wheels (Senior Meals Program)
programs.tagline: Free daily hot meals delivered to your parent's home if they have trouble shopping or cooking independently.

localEntities.name: SESS senior centers in Southeast Alaska

programs.intro:
If your parent is 60+ and has difficulty shopping for groceries or preparing meals due to mobility challenges, they may qualify for free daily hot meals delivered to their home. Each meal provides one-third of daily nutritional requirements, with frozen meals for weekends and holidays in some areas.

Unlike many senior programs, Meals on Wheels has no income limits — eligibility is based on age and physical need, not financial status. Because each meal is nutritionally balanced by dietitians, your parent gets consistent nutrition even when cooking becomes difficult.

Availability varies significantly by region in Alaska. Home delivery requires a doctor's note in some areas like North Slope Borough, while other regions like Anchorage through Salvation Army have simpler intake processes.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be physically limited in ability to independently carry out activities of daily living, such as mobility challenges making it hard to shop for food, prepare meals, or socialize
otherRequirements: Complete client profile or application with local provider
For home delivery in some areas: doctor's note stating homebound or disabled

applicationGuide.summary: Call your regional provider to complete a client assessment — no standardized application form exists statewide.

steps.title: Find your local provider
steps.description: Anchorage: Salvation Army (907) 349-0613. Fairbanks: Senior Center (907) 452-1735. North Slope: Borough Senior Program (907) 852-0276. Wasilla: WASI. Southeast Alaska: SESS senior centers.

steps.title: Complete intake assessment
steps.description: Provider will assess your parent's physical limitations and need for meal assistance. Some areas require doctor's note for home delivery.

steps.title: Submit required forms
steps.description: Complete client profile and any additional forms required by your local provider. Fairbanks uses MOW and Nutritional forms.

applicationGuide.tip: If no local provider serves your area, contact Alaska State Unit on Aging for referrals to nearby services.

documentsNeeded: Valid government-issued photo ID
Proof of age (birth certificate, driver's license, or passport)
Doctor's note stating homebound or disabled status (required for home delivery in North Slope Borough)
Referral letter from doctor or social worker (may be required by some providers)
Emergency contact information
List of current medications and dietary restrictions

contacts.label: Salvation Army Anchorage
contacts.description: Home-delivered and group meals for Anchorage area

contacts.label: Fairbanks Senior Center
contacts.description: Home-delivered meals for Fairbanks area

contacts.label: North Slope Borough Senior Program
contacts.description: Senior meals program requiring doctor's note for home delivery

contacts.label: Meals on Wheels America Finder
contacts.description: National directory to find local providers

applicationNotes: Requirements vary significantly by region — North Slope requires doctor's note for home delivery while other areas may not
Most programs accept suggested donations but meals remain free regardless of ability to pay
Congregate meal sites offer social interaction in addition to nutrition — ask about both options
If your parent needs immediate assistance, mention this during intake as some areas prioritize urgent cases

faqs.question: What if there's no Meals on Wheels provider in my parent's area?
faqs.answer: Contact the Alaska State Unit on Aging for referrals to nearby meal programs or alternative food assistance. Some areas may have food pantries or senior centers with meal programs that aren't officially Meals on Wheels. Rural areas often have limited options, but the state office maintains a directory of available services.

faqs.question: Can my parent get both home-delivered meals and go to congregate meal sites?
faqs.answer: This depends on your local provider's policies and your parent's assessed need. Most programs prioritize home delivery for those who cannot leave home safely, while encouraging mobile seniors to attend group meal sites for social interaction. Ask your provider about combining services or transitioning between them as needs change.

faqs.question: Does my parent have to pay for meals if they have retirement income?
faqs.answer: No income limits apply to Meals on Wheels in Alaska. Meals are provided regardless of your parent's financial situation. Most programs accept voluntary donations but never require payment. This makes it different from programs like SNAP that have strict income requirements.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent is temporarily hospitalized or travels?
faqs.answer: Contact your provider immediately to pause meal delivery. Most programs can hold your parent's spot and resume service when they return home. Don't let meals pile up or go to waste — providers need advance notice for scheduling and food safety. Some programs require re-assessment after extended absences.

faqs.question: How quickly can meal delivery start after approval?
faqs.answer: This varies by provider and current capacity. Some areas like Anchorage through Salvation Army may start within days of completing intake, while rural areas might have longer wait times due to limited delivery routes. Ask about timeline during your initial call and mention if your parent has urgent nutritional needs.

faqs.question: Can I apply for my parent if they have dementia or can't make phone calls?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can initiate the application process. However, the provider will likely need to assess your parent directly or speak with their doctor about care needs and safety. Bring documentation of your authority to act on their behalf, such as power of attorney, to streamline the process.

programs.name: Alzheimer's Family Caregiver Support Program
programs.tagline: Get respite care, counseling, and training when you're caring for a parent with Alzheimer's or dementia.

localEntities.name: Local grantees of Division of Senior and Disabilities Services

programs.intro:
If you're caring for a parent with Alzheimer's disease or related dementia in Alaska, you can access free respite care, counseling, training, and support services. The program provides both adult day care and in-home respite with no service caps, plus individual counseling, support groups, and help with home modifications or transportation.

Alaska operates two tracks: one specifically for Alzheimer's caregivers (requires diagnosis proof and income limits) and a broader program for families caring for anyone 60+ or grandparents raising grandchildren. Both provide the same core services — respite care, caregiver training, and emotional support — administered through local grantees across all Alaska communities.

otherRequirements: Care recipient must have formal diagnosis of Alzheimer's disease or related dementia
Caregiver must be providing unpaid care
Alternative track: Care recipient 60+ or caregiver 60+ caring for disabled adult child or grandparents 55+ raising grandchildren

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through local grantees or contact Alaska's Division of Senior and Disabilities Services — processing time not specified.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: You'll need proof of Alzheimer's diagnosis from a physician, completed application form, and proof of age if required.

steps.title: Contact your local grantee
steps.description: Visit http://dhss.alaska.gov/dsds/Pages/hcb/hcb.aspx to find local grantees of Alaska's Division of Senior and Disabilities Services in your area.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Complete the Caregiver Support Services Application (fillable form available from Anchorage Older Americans Services Commission) and submit to your local grantee.

steps.title: Assessment and approval
steps.description: Local staff will review your application, verify diagnosis and financial eligibility, and connect you with available services in your community.

applicationGuide.tip: If you're unsure which track applies to your situation, contact your local grantee — they can help determine whether you qualify under the Alzheimer's-specific program or the broader age-based program.

urls.label: Alaska Home & Community Based Senior Grants

documentsNeeded: Completed Caregiver Support Services Application form
Physician's note or medical record confirming Alzheimer's disease or related dementia diagnosis
Proof of age (driver's license, birth certificate, or state-issued ID)
Documentation of unpaid caregiver relationship
Proof of income for financial eligibility screening
Asset documentation for financial eligibility screening

contacts.label: Alaska Division of Senior and Disabilities Services
contacts.description: Primary program administrator - find local grantees through website

contacts.label: Alzheimer's Disease Resource Agency
contacts.description: Specialized services in four Alaska locations

applicationNotes: Two separate programs with different eligibility: income-tested Alzheimer's-specific track versus broader age-based track without financial limits
Spouses and legal guardians may be ineligible as paid caregivers in related programs — verify your unpaid caregiver status
Services vary by community and local grantee — ask about specific respite options and supplemental services available in your area
Don't confuse this with Alaska's ALI Waiver program, which has waitlists and different Medicaid-based eligibility requirements

faqs.question: Can I apply for both the Alzheimer's-specific program and the broader caregiver program?
faqs.answer: No, you'll be enrolled in whichever program you qualify for first. The Alzheimer's-specific track (CSRCP) has income and asset limits but serves all ages with dementia. The broader track (NFCSP) serves families where the care recipient is 60+ or other age-based criteria, without financial limits.

faqs.question: What if my parent has dementia but not a formal Alzheimer's diagnosis?
faqs.answer: Related dementias qualify for the CSRCP track, but you need official medical documentation. If you can't get formal diagnosis documentation, you may still qualify through the broader NFCSP track if your parent is 60+ or you meet other age requirements.

faqs.question: Is there a limit on how much respite care we can get?
faqs.answer: No service caps apply to core respite services including adult day care and in-home options. However, supplemental services like transportation and home modifications are limited and vary by local grantee availability.

faqs.question: What are the actual income and asset limits for the Alzheimer's program?
faqs.answer: The specific dollar amounts and household size tables aren't publicly detailed in program materials. Contact your local grantee for current financial eligibility thresholds, as these may vary and update annually.

faqs.question: Can I get paid as a family caregiver through this program?
faqs.answer: This program specifically requires unpaid caregiving. If you're looking for paid family caregiver opportunities, ask about Alaska's Medicaid waiver programs or other state programs that may offer stipends.

faqs.question: How do I find services if I live in rural Alaska?
faqs.answer: The program operates in 12 communities statewide through local grantees, with Alzheimer's Resource Agency in four locations. Contact the Division of Senior and Disabilities Services to find the closest grantee serving your area.

faqs.question: Can I apply while my parent is in the hospital or nursing home?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can apply regardless of where your parent currently receives care. The program is designed to support family caregivers and can help with transition planning and ongoing support even if care settings change.

faqs.question: What happens if our income changes after we're approved?
faqs.answer: For the income-tested Alzheimer's track, you'll need to report significant income changes. The broader NFCSP track doesn't have ongoing income monitoring. Contact your local grantee about reporting requirements and reassessment timelines.

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP) / Mature Alaskans Seeking Skills Training (MASST)
programs.tagline: Part-time paid work placement for unemployed Alaskans 55+ while building skills for permanent employment.

localEntities.name: Alaska Department of Labor and Workforce Development, Division of Vocational Rehabilitation
Non-profits, governmental agencies, and Native organizations

programs.intro:
If your parent is 55+ and struggling to find work, MASST provides part-time paid employment (averaging 20 hours per week at minimum wage) while they train for permanent jobs. Your parent works at community organizations — nonprofits, government agencies, or Native organizations — while receiving job search assistance and skills training.

Because this is a training program, not permanent employment, your parent has a 48-month lifetime limit to participate. The program requires family income below 125% of the federal poverty level and prioritizes veterans, those over 65, people with disabilities, and rural residents.

Most participants transition to unsubsidized employment before their time limit expires. The program operates statewide through regional coordinators who match participants with local placements.

otherRequirements: Must be unemployed
Must have poor employment prospects

Priority given to veterans, those over 65, people with disabilities, those with low literacy or limited English, rural residents, homeless individuals, and those unsuccessful with American Job Center services

applicationGuide.summary: Apply in person at Alaska Job Center Network locations — processing times not specified by the program.

steps.title: Visit Alaska Job Center Network location
steps.description: Go to your nearest AJCN office to apply in person. Locations operate statewide.

steps.title: Contact Division of Vocational Rehabilitation
steps.description: Reach out to Alaska Department of Labor and Workforce Development, Division of Vocational Rehabilitation for application assistance.

steps.title: Complete enrollment paperwork
steps.description: Fill out application forms including Alaska SCSEP Durational Limits Form that explains the 48-month participation limit.

steps.title: Work with regional coordinator
steps.description: Your regional coordinator will match you with community service placements at nonprofits, government agencies, or Native organizations.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Priority enrollment system may create delays for non-priority applicants
applicationGuide.tip: Veterans and those over 65 get priority — mention veteran status or advanced age when applying.

documentsNeeded: Valid government-issued photo ID
Social Security card
Proof of age (birth certificate or passport)
Proof of Alaska residency (utility bill, lease agreement, or state-issued document)
Income documentation for all household members (last 30 days of pay stubs, Social Security award letters, pension statements)
Proof of unemployment status
Military discharge papers (DD-214) if veteran
Documentation of disability if applicable
Proof of English proficiency level if limited English speaker
Documentation of previous job search efforts or American Job Center services if applicable

contacts.label: Alaska Department of Labor and Workforce Development
contacts.description: Division of Vocational Rehabilitation administers MASST statewide

contacts.label: Alaska 2-1-1
contacts.description: Free helpline for information about social services including employment programs

applicationNotes: Priority enrollment means veterans, those over 65, people with disabilities, and rural residents may be served first even if others applied earlier
The 48-month lifetime participation limit has no exceptions or waivers — plan for transition to unsubsidized employment
Program provides part-time work only (average 20 hours/week) — not suitable for those needing full-time income immediately
Regional coordinators handle placements differently across Alaska — ask about typical wait times and available positions in your area
relatedPrograms: Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)

faqs.question: Can my parent participate in MASST more than once?
faqs.answer: No. There is a 48-month lifetime participation limit with no exceptions or waivers. Once your parent uses their 48 months, they cannot re-enroll in the program at any point in the future.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent can't find regular employment before the 48-month limit?
faqs.answer: The program requires participants to actively seek unsubsidized employment throughout their participation. Job placement assistance is provided to help transition before the limit expires. However, there are no extensions available.

faqs.question: How much will my parent earn in the MASST program?
faqs.answer: Participants earn the highest of federal, state, or local minimum wage for an average of 20 hours per week. The exact amount depends on current minimum wage rates in Alaska and the specific number of hours worked each week.

faqs.question: Does veteran status really help with enrollment priority?
faqs.answer: Yes. Veterans and qualified spouses receive enrollment priority along with those over 65, people with disabilities, and several other categories. Make sure to bring DD-214 discharge papers when applying.

faqs.question: Can my parent apply for MASST while receiving Social Security or other benefits?
faqs.answer: Possibly, but the income from those benefits counts toward the 125% federal poverty level limit. Your parent must meet the total household income requirement regardless of benefit sources.

faqs.question: What kind of work placements are available through MASST?
faqs.answer: Placements are with nonprofits, government agencies, and Native organizations throughout Alaska. Regional coordinators match participants with available positions, which vary by location and community needs.

faqs.question: Is there job training included or just work placement?
faqs.answer: Yes. The program provides on-the-job training in computer and vocational skills, plus job search assistance and professional placement help. Training is designed to prepare participants for unsubsidized employment.

faqs.question: What if my parent lives in rural Alaska — is MASST available?
faqs.answer: MASST operates statewide and rural residents actually receive priority consideration for enrollment. Regional coordinators work to arrange placements throughout Alaska, including rural areas.

programs.name: Alaska Legal Services Corporation (ALSC) Senior Legal Hotline
Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program
programs.tagline: Free advocacy when your parent faces problems in their nursing home or assisted living facility.

localEntities.name: Main office: 3745 Community Park Loop, Suite 200, Anchorage, AK 99508

programs.intro:
If your parent is 60+ and living in a nursing home or assisted living facility in Alaska, the Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program provides free advocacy when problems arise. Certified ombudsmen investigate complaints about health, safety, welfare, or rights violations — from poor care to billing disputes to facility policies that seem unfair.

Because this is an independent advocacy program, ombudsmen can take action on your parent's behalf without conflicts of interest. They visit all skilled nursing facilities and licensed assisted living homes at least once per year and respond to specific complaints promptly. The program covers all of Alaska through a central office in Anchorage and trained volunteers statewide.

No income limits apply — any resident or family member can contact the program for help with facility-related issues.

summary: Living in Alaska long-term care facility
Any complaint about health, safety, or rights
otherRequirements: Must be residing in a long-term care facility such as an assisted living home or nursing home in Alaska
Complaint or issue must relate to health, safety, welfare, or rights in the facility

applicationGuide.summary: Call 907-334-4480 or use the online complaint form at akoltco.org — no formal application needed.

steps.title: Contact the ombudsman
steps.description: Call 907-334-4480 or toll-free 1-800-730-6393 (Monday-Friday 8:00 a.m. to 4:30 p.m.) or submit an online complaint form at akoltco.org

steps.title: Describe the issue
steps.description: Provide the facility name, your parent's name, and a description of the complaint or concern

steps.title: Investigation begins
steps.description: A certified ombudsman will investigate the complaint and work toward resolution with the facility

applicationGuide.processingTime: Complaint investigations handled promptly by certified ombudsmen
applicationGuide.tip: Keep notes about specific incidents including dates, times, and staff involved — this helps the ombudsman investigate more effectively.

contacts.label: Alaska Long-Term Care Ombudsman
contacts.description: Main ombudsman office for complaints and advocacy

contacts.label: Toll-free ombudsman line
contacts.description: Statewide toll-free access to ombudsman services

faqs.question: Can I file a complaint if my parent doesn't want me to?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can contact the ombudsman with concerns. However, the ombudsman must get your parent's permission before investigating on their behalf, unless there are immediate safety concerns that require intervention.

faqs.question: What if the facility retaliates against my parent for filing a complaint?
faqs.answer: Retaliation is illegal and a serious violation. Contact the ombudsman immediately at 907-334-4480 if you suspect retaliation. They can investigate retaliation claims and take action to protect your parent's rights.

faqs.question: Will filing a complaint with the ombudsman affect other legal action?
faqs.answer: The ombudsman program focuses on resolving issues through advocacy, not legal proceedings. You can pursue both ombudsman assistance and legal action simultaneously — they serve different purposes and don't conflict with each other.

stateOverview.intro: If you're helping an aging parent in Alaska, 12 state and federal programs can provide financial assistance, health coverage, and support services. These range from Medicaid covering comprehensive health care and nursing home costs, to SNAP providing $100-300+ monthly for groceries, to free daily meal delivery through Meals on Wheels. Alaska administers most benefits through the Division of Public Assistance, with additional support through the Alaska Commission on Aging. Start by checking the highest-value programs first — Medicaid for health coverage, Medicare Savings Programs to reduce Medicare costs, and SNAP for food assistance — then explore specialized services like home care waivers or caregiver support programs.

startHere.name: Medicaid for Seniors/Disabled
startHere.why: Covers comprehensive health care including nursing home care and home-based services for the highest medical costs your parent faces.

startHere.name: Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
startHere.why: Reduces Medicare premiums, deductibles, and copays if your parent's income is below $2,245/month.

startHere.name: Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP)
startHere.why: Provides hundreds of dollars monthly for groceries — one of the most immediate financial relief programs for seniors on fixed incomes.

byNeed.description: Programs that cover health insurance, reduce Medicare costs, or provide free help navigating medical coverage options.

byNeed.need: Staying at home instead of nursing care
programs: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
byNeed.description: Services that provide home care, medical coordination, and support to help your parent remain in their own home.
Monthly grocery benefits or free home-delivered meals for seniors who have trouble shopping or cooking.

programs: Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)
byNeed.description: Help paying heating bills and free weatherization services to reduce energy costs in Alaska's harsh climate.
Respite care and training for family caregivers, plus free advocacy for nursing home or assisted living problems.

programs: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP) / Mature Alaskans Seeking Skills Training (MASST)
byNeed.description: Part-time paid work placement for unemployed Alaskans 55+ while building skills for permanent employment.

quickFacts: Alaska does not currently have a PACE provider operating in the state, limiting all-in-one medical care options for seniors
Most benefit applications go through Alaska's Division of Public Assistance online portal or local offices
Alaska's high cost of living may mean higher benefit amounts for programs like SNAP compared to other states
The Alaska Commission on Aging coordinates many senior services and can help connect you to local Area Agencies on Aging
stateOverview.resourcesVsBenefits: Nine programs require qualifying based on your parent's income, assets, or medical needs — like Medicaid (income limits), SNAP (fixed income requirements), or Home and Community-Based Services (needing daily care assistance). Three programs are free resources available to any Alaska senior: SHIP provides free Medicare counseling regardless of income, and the Long-Term Care Ombudsman offers free advocacy in care facilities. The Alzheimer's Family Caregiver Support Program provides respite and training when you're caring for a parent with dementia.
